Read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md (integration rules) and the relevant sections of /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md first — this round's clean-room work aimed to close exactly the blockers documented there.
RULES: adapters and extraction only — NEVER transplant ported logic into clean files (a clean file acquiring a GPL header from scripts/gen_license_headers.py is a provenance violation: stop and report it). Vendored clean sources get mechanical fixes only (crate:: path adjustments, include paths); they stay headerless. If a gap still blocks a swap, KEEP the ported path with its header and report the gap precisely — never force it. Code comments describe current code only. Do not run git commits. Return a plain-text summary; append a new dated section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md with per-unit deleted/kept/header-delta detail.
VALIDATION (at the end): cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-parser -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-prover -p tamarin-server; the wellformedness fixture suite; scripts/gen_license_headers.py followed by --check (expect 0 stale).

Integrate the round-5 clean-room closures for units B (graph) and A (web) in crates/tamarin-server. Similarity audits PASSED for: B, A. FAILED (do NOT integrate these — keep-and-report): (none). NOTE: a theory-side integrator (units C/D/E/G) ran before you and is finished; rebase your expectations on the CURRENT tree, and re-run the full validation yourself.
- B: re-sync graph_clean from /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/workspace/graph-clean (mechanical fixes only, headerless). Route system_to_dot / system_to_dot_with / render_svg_or_dot_with through graph_clean generate — the new pre-rendered-cell entry with the ported term printer as cell provider is the intended adapter seam (the ported pretty-printer belongs to the theory layer and stays ported+headered; that is fine). BYTE GATE: the in-repo captured HS graph fixtures are ISE pages, so build a LIVE gate: use the graphdot cluster's reference-server recipes (PATH needs /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin; ports 3200-3299) to capture fresh reference DOT for a probe set (clustered/role-annotated, wide-cell, induction, compressed and uncompressed variants) and require the Rust route output byte-equal; keep the GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS round-trip green. Update routes_graph::dot_output_for_a_simple_system from the ported dialect to the reference dialect. When green, DELETE handlers/dot.rs's serialization, graph/abbreviation.rs, graph/repr.rs, graph/simplify.rs, graph/options.rs — whatever the clean modules now cover; keep-and-report any residue precisely. Then rename graph_clean -> graph (update paths/imports; suffix-free).
- A: adopt web_clean::dispatch::Server as the single request path. Implement ProverOps by EXTRACTING the fragment/data producers from handlers/theory.rs, theory_html.rs, handlers/proof_tree.rs into pure functions — extraction/refactor of ported code is allowed; extracted code stays in ported (headered) files. Route ALL routes through Server (its Route::parse now covers the full surface) with axum reduced to transport glue; migrate version/theory state into Server's state machine. Gate: ALL route tests + captured-HS parity fixtures green (update axum glue tests as needed, never weaken byte assertions). When the ported dispatch/state files (routes.rs routing, state.rs version logic, handler dispatch shells) are no longer referenced, DELETE them. Then rename web_clean -> web (suffix-free).
Stop any live servers you start. Expect the header count to DROP this round; report the exact delta and which upstream authors' citations disappeared.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

Reproducing byte-exact HS ids requires 129 that allocation logic, which lives in the ported serializer being "deleted" 130 — writing it into the adapter would transplant replaced logic. 131 132 Consequence: a rewire that kept the ported cell renderer + abbreviation + 133 missing-node fallback + id scheme would GROW `handlers/dot.rs` (adapter on top 134 of a still-live serializer) rather than thin it, and would not be byte-faithful 135 — the opposite of the task's goal. Downstream `cargo build -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-server` clean. 203 204 ### C.2 check_terms.rs / check_guarded_wf residue — KEPT, gaps reported 205 The task's target rewire (route `check_terms::check_terms_wf`'s formula-terms 206 through `wf::formula_terms_reducible`, and the `elaborate::check_guarded_wf` call 207 sites through the clean two-mode guardedness) is NOT performed: both clean 208 entry points have an ORACLE-CONFIRMED behavior gap that a thin adapter cannot 209 close without transplanting the replaced sort-kind logic. (K(x) @ #x) ==> F" 219 prints `Lemma `L' uses terms of the wrong form: `Free x'` 220 because the node binder `#x` does NOT bind the message-position use `x`. The 221 clean name-only model binds it (`Bound 0`) and reports NO offender — a silent 222 regression. The ported `check_terms.rs` matches by name AND sort-kind AND idx 223 (`lookup_bound`/`kind_of`) and reproduces the oracle byte-exactly; its unit test 224 `untagged_message_use_does_not_bind_to_node_binder` is the captured case and 225 stays green. The round-3 clean checks DO ship 243 in the parser-level `wf::check_theory` (pre-elaboration, fixture-validated). === hs_server.sh ===
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Black-box oracle: serve a .spthy in the HS interactive web UI.
# Usage: hs_server.sh start <file.spthy> <port>   (then curl http://127.0.0.1:<port>/...)
#        hs_server.sh stop <port>
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
case "$1" in
  start) d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/"; nohup "/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover" interactive "$d" --port "$3" >/tmp/hs_server_$3.log 2>&1 & echo $! > /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid; sleep 4; echo "up on $3 (pid $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid))";;
  stop) kill $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid) 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid; echo stopped;;
esac

=== wl_server4.sh ===
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Live reference-server wrapper for UNIT A round 4 probing.
# Usage: wl_server4.sh start <file.spthy> <port> [extra tamarin flags...]
#        wl_server4.sh stop <port>
# Uses the --port=<n> (equals) form to avoid the workdir-positional bug.
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
BIN="/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover"
cmd="$1"; shift
case "$cmd" in
  start)
    f="$1"; port="$2"; shift 2
    d=$(mktemp -d)
    cp "$f" "$d/"
    nohup "$BIN" interactive "$d" --port="$port" "$@" \
      >"/tmp/wl4_$port.log" 2>&1 &
    echo $! > "/tmp/wl4_$port.pid"
    sleep 5
    echo "up on $port (pid $(cat /tmp/wl4_$port.pid)) dir $d"
    ;;
  stop)
    port="$1"
    kill "$(cat /tmp/wl4_$port.pid)" 2>/dev/null
    rm -f "/tmp/wl4_$port.pid"
    echo "stopped $port"
    ;;
esac

2	//   meiersi, jdreier, addap, Mathias-AURAND, arcz, rkunnema, beschmi,
3	//   felixlinker, cascremers, YannColomb, rsasse, Kanakanajm,
4	//   PhilipLukertWork, sans-sucre, yavivanov, BTom-GH, ValentinYuri,
5	//   symphorien, xaDxelA, Hong-Thai, kevinmorio, charlie-j, and other
6	//   minor contributors (see upstream git history)
7	// Ported from upstream tamarin-prover sources:
8	//   lib/term/src/Term/LTerm.hs,
9	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Constraints.hs,
10	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Dot.hs,
11	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Graph/Graph.hs,
12	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Graph/GraphRepr.hs,
13	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Model/Fact.hs,
14	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Model/Rule.hs,
15	//   lib/theory/src/Theory/Text/Parser/Fact.hs,
16	//   lib/utils/src/Control/Monad/Disj/Class.hs,
17	//   lib/utils/src/Text/Dot.hs, lib/utils/src/Text/PrettyPrint/Class.hs,
18	//   src/Web/Handler.hs
19	
20	//! Port of Haskell's `Theory.Constraint.System.Dot` +
21	//! `Theory.Constraint.System.Graph.*` — convert a `System` into a
22	//! Graphviz DOT representation suitable for `dot -Tsvg`.
23	//!
24	//! We render the same kinds of nodes / edges / clusters as a single
25	//! self-contained DOT document, including an HTML-table legend for the
26	//! chosen abbreviations and similar-name / role clustering. Node ids,
27	//! fact rendering (`prettyLNFact`), action-row filtering (Diff /
28	//! auto-source), the cluster/preamble attribute blocks, the `roleColor`
29	//! cluster styling and the less-edge rendering all match HS byte-for-
30	//! byte.
31	//!
32	//! Per-rule node FILL colours are a faithful port of HS `nodeColorMap`
33	//! (Dot.hs:190-218): the size-dependent light-HSV palette keyed by
34	//! `(groupIdx, memberIdx)` — see `build_node_color_map` / `NodeColorMap`
35	//! below. An explicit per-rule `color:` attribute and a cluster's
36	//! `manualNodeColor` still take priority (HS `dotNodeCompact`, Dot.hs:248-256).
37	//! Each rule record also carries HS's `fontcolor` (`colorUsesWhiteFont` of the
38	//! palette colour, Dot.hs:258/284-287) and `role` (Dot.hs:259) attributes.
39	//!
40	//! KNOWN DIVERGENCES:
41	//!   * (serialization form only — normalised away by the parse-and-compare
42	//!     gate) the cluster subgraph identifier uses the Rust `cluster_<n>` form
43	//!     rather than HS `createClusterNodeId roleName`. The cluster's label /
44	//!     colour / membership are all faithful.
45	//!   * HS `mkNode`'s `CompactBoringNodes` branch (Dot.hs:294-304) — PORTED
46	//!     (see `rule_node`): under the default node style, intruder rules and the
47	//!     `Fresh` rule collapse to a PLAIN `mkSimpleNode` ellipse (Dot.hs:289-290)
48	//!     with no fill/font/role attrs. The label is `show v : showDotRuleCaseName
49	//!     ru` when the node has an outgoing edge (`hasOutgoingEdge`, Dot.hs:277-279,
50	//!     over the TOP-LEVEL `grEdges` only), else the full rule label incl. the
51	//!     bracketed action row. The `uncompact`/`FullBoringNodes` toggle is not
52	//!     plumbed through the RS handler (see `graph/options.rs`), so this route is
53	//!     always compact — matching the HS default (`defaultDotOptions`, Dot.hs:82).
54	//!   * SERIALIZATION form only (normalised away by the parse-and-compare gate):
55	//!     protocol-rule RECORD labels use RS port ids `<p0>`/`<c0>` and spaced
56	//!     `{ .. } | .. | { .. }` bracketing, where HS's `Text.Dot.renderRecord`
57	//!     (Dot.hs:254-280) uses a graph-global port counter `<n0>`, `<n1>`, … and
58	//!     `{{..|..}|{..}|{..|..}}` bracketing. The gate ignores the node-id scheme
59	//!     and record bracketing; the field CONTENT (facts, `id : name[acts]`) is
60	//!     rendered identically.
61	//!
62	//! Reference:
63	//!   - `lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Dot.hs` (605 lines)
64	//!   - `lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Graph/Graph.hs`
65	//!   - `lib/theory/src/Theory/Constraint/System/Graph/GraphRepr.hs`
66	//!
67	//! The shape mirrors `systemToGraph` + `dotSystemCompact`:
68	//!   1. Collect nodes from `sNodes`, plus "missing" nodes referenced
69	//!      by edges but absent from `sNodes`.
70	//!   2. Add unsolved-action-atom nodes (KU goals at fresh ids).
71	//!   3. Add the `LastAtom` node, if any.
72	//!   4. Emit edges from `sEdges` (conclusion → premise) styled by
73	//!      fact tag.
74	//!   5. Emit less-edges from `sLessAtoms` (dashed, coloured by
75	//!      reason).
76	//!   6. Emit chain edges from unsolved Chain goals (dotted green).
77	//!
78	//! Each rule node is rendered as a Graphviz record:
79	//!
80	//! ```text
81	//!     +------------+------------+
82	//!     |  prem_0    |  prem_1    |
83	//!     +------------+------------+
84	//!     |     <#i> : RuleName     |
85	//!     +------------+------------+
86	//!     |  conc_0    |  conc_1    |
87	//!     +------------+------------+
88	//! ```
89	//!
90	//! with port names `p0`, `p1`, ..., `c0`, `c1`, ... so that edges from
91	//! the `sEdges` set can target the correct slots.
92	
93	// every `HashMap`/`HashSet` in this DOT renderer is a
94	// keyed-lookup / membership helper — `node_map` (node id -> rule, `.get`),
95	// `has_outgoing` / `port_owner_ids` / `used_dot_ids` (`.contains` / `.insert`
96	// dedup).  DOT output ORDER is driven by iterating the ordered
97	// `repr.nodes` / `repr.edges` Vecs; these maps/sets are never iterated into
98	// output.  Also off the batch `--prove` byte-parity surface (server graph UI).
99	// std kept (byte-inert).
100	#![allow(clippy::disallowed_types)]
101	
102	use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
103	use std::fmt::Write as _;
104	
105	use tamarin_theory::constraint::constraints::{LessAtom, NodeId, Reason};
106	use tamarin_theory::constraint::system::System;
107	use tamarin_theory::fact::{FactTag, LNFact};
108	use tamarin_theory::pretty_hpj::{self, Doc, WEB_LINE_LENGTH, WEB_RIBBON};
109	use tamarin_theory::rule::{
110	    rule_name_string, IntrRuleACInfo, ProtoRuleName, RuleACInst, RuleInfo,
111	};
112	use tamarin_term::lterm::{LNTerm, LVar};
113	use tamarin_term::pretty::pretty_lnterm;
114	// `fix_multi_line_label` is HS `fixMultiLineLabel` (Text/Dot.hs:355-363),
115	// applied to every record FIELD by the `mkField` smart constructor
116	// (Text/Dot.hs:378-381): a multi-line label has each line's leading spaces
117	// replaced 1:1 by ` ` and is re-joined with `unlines` — which appends a
118	// TRAILING newline (→ a trailing `\l` after `showAttr`).  Single-line labels
119	// pass through untouched.
120	use tamarin_utils::dot::fix_multi_line_label;
121	
122	use crate::graph::abbreviation::{
123	    apply_abbreviations_fact, compute_abbreviations, AbbreviationOptions,
124	    Abbreviations,
125	};
126	use crate::graph::options::GraphOptions;
127	use crate::graph::repr::{
128	    add_cluster_by_role, add_intelligent_cluster_using_similar_names,
129	    compute_basic_graph_repr, extract_base_name, extract_role, GEdge, GNode,
130	    MissingHint, NodeType,
131	};
132	use crate::graph::simplify::{compress_system, simplify_system};
133	use crate::graph::render_system::RenderSystem;
134	
135	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------
136	// Public API
137	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------
138	
139	/// Render a [`System`] into a Graphviz DOT document with default
140	/// graph options (Haskell `defaultGraphOptions`: SL2 + compress).
141	/// Returns a self-contained `digraph G { ... }` block.
142	pub fn system_to_dot(sys: &System) -> String {
143	    system_to_dot_with(sys, &GraphOptions::default())
144	}
145	
146	/// Render a [`System`] into a Graphviz DOT document under the given
147	/// options.  Applies compression, simplification, role-clustering, and
148	/// abbreviation discovery before emitting DOT, mirroring Haskell's
149	/// `systemToGraph` + `dotSystemCompact`.
150	pub fn system_to_dot_with(sys: &System, opts: &GraphOptions) -> String {
151	    // 1. Pre-render simplification.  Clone-for-render boundary: from here on the
152	    //    working copy is a `RenderSystem` (display-only, write-sealed) so it can
153	    //    never be fed back into the prover — the compress/simplify passes mutate
154	    //    it in ways that leave the `subst_system` stamps meaningless.
155	    let working = RenderSystem::from_prover(sys.clone());
156	    let working = if opts.compress { compress_system(working) } else { working };
157	    let working = simplify_system(opts.simplification_level, working);
158	    // 2. Build the GraphRepr.  `compute_basic_graph_repr` takes `&System`;
159	    //    `&RenderSystem` derefs to it.
160	    let mut repr = compute_basic_graph_repr(&working);
161	    if opts.clustering_similar_names {
162	        add_intelligent_cluster_using_similar_names(&mut repr);
163	    } else {
164	        add_cluster_by_role(&mut repr);
165	    }
166	    // 3. Compute abbreviations.
167	    let abbrevs: Abbreviations = if opts.abbreviate {
168	        compute_abbreviations(&repr, &AbbreviationOptions::default())
169	    } else {
170	        Abbreviations::new()
171	    };
172	    // 4. Emit DOT.
173	    let mut g = DotBuilder::new();
174	    // HS `dotSystemCompact` (Dot.hs:481-487) computes the node colour map from
175	    // the RAW system's nodes (`nodeColorMap (M.elems $ get sNodes se)`), NOT
176	    // the compressed/simplified `working` used for the graph. Mirror that: the
177	    // palette is sized by the whole rule set, so it must see every original
178	    // node.
179	    let color_map = build_node_color_map(&sys.nodes);
180	    // HS `dotGraphCompact` (Dot.hs:503) switches the graph-level defaults to
181	    // `setDefaultAttributesIfCluster` when the repr has any clusters.
182	    g.preamble(!repr.clusters.is_empty());
183	    let abbrev_lookup = |t: &LNTerm| -> Option<LNTerm> {
184	        abbrevs.get(t).map(|(a, _)| a.clone())
185	    };
186	    // Precompute a node-id -> rule map so edge styling is O(1) per edge
187	    // instead of scanning `working.nodes` per edge.
188	    let node_map: HashMap<&LVar, &RuleACInst> =
189	        working.nodes.iter().map(|(id, ru)| (id, ru)).collect();
190	    // HS `hasOutgoingEdge graph v` (Dot.hs:277-279): a node has an outgoing edge
191	    // iff it is the conclusion-side source of some `SystemEdge` in the graph's
192	    // TOP-LEVEL edge set (`get grEdges repr`). Clustering removes a cluster's
193	    // internal edges from `grEdges` (GraphRepr.hs:126-129), so we mirror HS and
194	    // consult ONLY `repr.edges` (post-clustering), never a cluster's own edges.
195	    // Drives the compact-node label choice in `rule_node`.
196	    let has_outgoing: HashSet<&LVar> = repr.edges.iter()
197	        .filter_map(|e| match e {
198	            GEdge::System(src, _) => Some(&src.0),
199	            _ => None,
200	        })
201	        .collect();
202	    // HS gives every node a globally-fresh dot id via `cacheState dsNodes`
203	    // (Dot.hs:108-110), so a single system-node id `v` that is ALSO an
204	    // unsolved-action atom and/or the last-action atom is drawn as SEVERAL
205	    // distinct dot nodes (`n5` record + `n7` ellipse …). RS's semantic dot-id
206	    // scheme derives one id per `v` (`dot_node_id`), so those extra ellipses
207	    // would collide with the record on a single id — graphviz merges them (the
208	    // ellipse overwrites the record) and the parity gate's label-keyed node map
209	    // drops the record. Mirror HS: the SystemNode / MissingNode own the base id
210	    // (their record ports / bare id are what `sEdges` reference via
211	    // `conc_port_ref`/`prem_port_ref` = HS `dsConcs`/`dsPrems`), and a colliding
212	    // UnsolvedAction / LastAction ellipse (which HS never references by an
213	    // sEdge) gets a distinct suffixed id.
214	    //
215	    // `dsNodes[v]` (which HS `dotLessEdge` resolves each less-edge endpoint
216	    // through, Dot.hs:408-409) is the LAST dot node emitted at `v`. Emission
217	    // order is the free `repr.nodes` (System, UnsolvedAction, LastAction,
218	    // Missing per `compute_basic_graph_repr`) then each cluster's nodes — so we
219	    // walk that exact order, assigning ids and overwriting `ds_nodes` as we go.
220	    //
221	    // `port_owner_ids` = the `v`s whose base id backs an sEdge port ref
222	    // (SystemNode records + MissingNodes). An UnsolvedAction/LastAction at such
223	    // a `v` MUST yield the base to that owner regardless of emission order (a
224	    // SystemNode can be clustered and thus emitted AFTER a free action ellipse).
225	    let port_owner_ids: HashSet<&LVar> = repr.nodes.iter()
226	        .chain(repr.clusters.iter().flat_map(|c| c.nodes.iter()))
227	        .filter(|n| matches!(n.ty, NodeType::System(_) | NodeType::Missing(_)))
228	        .map(|n| &n.id)
229	        .collect();
230	    let mut used_dot_ids: HashSet<String> = HashSet::new();
231	    // Assigned id for each UnsolvedAction (tag 0) / LastAction (tag 1) node,
232	    // keyed by (node id, tag) — at most one of each kind per `v`.
233	    let mut ellipse_dot_ids: std::collections::BTreeMap<(LVar, u8), String> =
234	        std::collections::BTreeMap::new();
235	    // `dsNodes`: v -> dot id of the LAST node emitted at v (less-edge target).
236	    let mut ds_nodes: std::collections::BTreeMap<LVar, String> =
237	        std::collections::BTreeMap::new();
238	    for node in repr.nodes.iter()
239	        .chain(repr.clusters.iter().flat_map(|c| c.nodes.iter()))
240	    {
241	        let base = DotBuilder::dot_node_id(&node.id);
242	        let id = match &node.ty {
243	            // Base-id owners: their id is referenced by sEdge port refs.
244	            NodeType::System(_) | NodeType::Missing(_) => {
245	                used_dot_ids.insert(base.clone());
246	                base
247	            }
248	            NodeType::UnsolvedAction(_) | NodeType::LastAction => {
249	                let tag: u8 = if matches!(node.ty, NodeType::LastAction) { 1 } else { 0 };
250	                let suffix = if tag == 1 { "__lastatom" } else { "__actionatom" };
251	                let id = if port_owner_ids.contains(&node.id)
252	                    || used_dot_ids.contains(&base)
253	                {
254	                    let mut cand = format!("{base}{suffix}");
255	                    let mut n = 2u32;
256	                    while used_dot_ids.contains(&cand) {
257	                        cand = format!("{base}{suffix}{n}");
258	                        n += 1;
259	                    }
260	                    cand
261	                } else {
262	                    base
263	                };
264	                used_dot_ids.insert(id.clone());
265	                ellipse_dot_ids.insert((node.id.clone(), tag), id.clone());
266	                id
267	            }
268	        };
269	        ds_nodes.insert(node.id.clone(), id);
270	    }
271	    // 4a. Top-level (ungrouped) nodes.
272	    //
273	    // HS `dotGraphCompact` (Dot.hs:505-510) emits, in order: the FREE
274	    // (ungrouped) nodes (`mapM_ dotNodeCompact nodes`), THEN the clusters
275	    // (`mapM_ dotCluster clusters`), THEN the edges.  The free nodes — e.g. an
276	    // unsolved-action-atom ellipse like `Unlock_0(..) @ #t2.1` — therefore
277	    // appear BEFORE any `subgraph cluster_*` block.  Emit them first to match
278	    // (a free node emitted after the cluster's closing `}` lands in the wrong
279	    // scope order vs HS).
280	    for node in &repr.nodes {
281	        emit_node(&mut g, node, &abbrev_lookup, opts, &color_map, &has_outgoing,
282	            &ellipse_dot_ids);
283	    }
284	    // 4b. Clusters as subgraphs.
285	    //
286	    // HS `dotCluster` (Dot.hs:547-562): each cluster gets a `roleColor`
287	    // derived from `extractBaseName name`, the subgraph is `style=filled`
288	    // with that colour, and the colour is threaded to the child nodes as
289	    // their `manualNodeColor` (Dot.hs:562). HS also defers ALL of a
290	    // cluster's edges to `dotClustersEdges` (Dot.hs:507-510/517-522), which
291	    // runs `mergeLessEdges` over the concatenation of every cluster's edges
292	    // and emits them AFTER every node/cluster — so we collect them here.
293	    let mut cluster_edges: Vec<GEdge> = Vec::new();
294	    for (i, cluster) in repr.clusters.iter().enumerate() {
295	        // `baseName = fromMaybe "Undefined" (extractBaseName name)`.
296	        let base = extract_base_name(&cluster.name)
297	            .unwrap_or_else(|| "Undefined".to_string());
298	        let color = role_color(&base);
299	        g.open_subgraph(i, &cluster.name, &color);
300	        for node in &cluster.nodes {
301	            emit_node_colored(&mut g, node, &abbrev_lookup, opts, Some(&color),
302	                &color_map, &has_outgoing, &ellipse_dot_ids);
303	        }
304	        g.close_subgraph();
305	        cluster_edges.extend(cluster.edges.iter().cloned());
306	    }
307	    // 4c. Edges. HS emits `restEdges` (non-less) before the merged
308	    // `lessEdges` within each scope (`dotGraphCompact`, Dot.hs:508-509),
309	    // then the cluster edges last (`dotClustersEdges`).
310	    emit_edges_merged(&mut g, &repr.edges, &node_map, &ds_nodes);
311	    emit_edges_merged(&mut g, &cluster_edges, &node_map, &ds_nodes);
312	    // 4d. Legend (if any abbreviations were chosen).
313	    if !abbrevs.is_empty() {
314	        g.legend(&abbrevs);
315	    }
316	    g.close();
317	    g.into_string()
318	}
319	
320	fn emit_node(
321	    g: &mut DotBuilder,
322	    node: &GNode,
323	    abbrev: &dyn Fn(&LNTerm) -> Option<LNTerm>,
324	    opts: &GraphOptions,
325	    color_map: &NodeColorMap,
326	    has_outgoing: &HashSet<&LVar>,
327	    ellipse_dot_ids: &std::collections::BTreeMap<(LVar, u8), String>,
328	) {
329	    emit_node_colored(g, node, abbrev, opts, None, color_map, has_outgoing,
330	        ellipse_dot_ids);
331	}
332	
333	/// `emit_node` with an optional `manual_color` — the cluster `roleColor`
334	/// that HS `dotCluster` threads to its child nodes as `manualNodeColor`
335	/// (Dot.hs:562). Only the `SystemNode` branch consults it (HS
336	/// `dotNodeCompact`, Dot.hs:248-256); the other node kinds ignore it.
337	fn emit_node_colored(
338	    g: &mut DotBuilder,
339	    node: &GNode,
340	    abbrev: &dyn Fn(&LNTerm) -> Option<LNTerm>,
341	    opts: &GraphOptions,
342	    manual_color: Option<&str>,
343	    color_map: &NodeColorMap,
344	    has_outgoing: &HashSet<&LVar>,
345	    ellipse_dot_ids: &std::collections::BTreeMap<(LVar, u8), String>,
346	) {
347	    // Look up the (possibly collision-disambiguated) dot id assigned to a
348	    // non-record ellipse node (UnsolvedAction tag 0 / LastAction tag 1).
349	    let ellipse_id = |tag: u8| -> String {
350	        ellipse_dot_ids.get(&(node.id.clone(), tag)).cloned()
351	            .unwrap_or_else(|| DotBuilder::dot_node_id(&node.id))
352	    };
353	    match &node.ty {
354	        NodeType::System(ru) => {
355	            let ru_abbreviated = abbreviate_rule(ru, abbrev);
356	            let outgoing = has_outgoing.contains(&node.id);
357	            g.rule_node(&node.id, &ru_abbreviated, opts, manual_color, color_map,
358	                outgoing);
359	        }
360	        NodeType::UnsolvedAction(facts) => {
361	            let new_facts: Vec<LNFact> = facts.iter()
362	                .map(|fa| apply_abbreviations_fact(abbrev, fa))
363	                .collect();
364	            // A colliding action ellipse (same `v` as a system record) gets a
365	            // distinct dot id so both nodes survive (see `ds_nodes`).
366	            g.action_node(&ellipse_id(0), &node.id, &new_facts);
367	        }
368	        // The last-atom uses its (possibly collision-disambiguated) dot id so
369	        // it does not clash with a same-id system node.
370	        NodeType::LastAction => {
371	            g.last_node(&ellipse_id(1), &node.id);
372	        }
373	        NodeType::Missing(hint) => g.missing_node(&node.id, hint),
374	    }
375	}
376	
377	/// Emit a scope's edges in HS `dotGraphCompact` order: every non-less edge
378	/// first (`restEdges`), then the merged less-edges (`mergeLessEdges`,
379	/// Dot.hs:567-597). Because `LessAtom` equality ignores the reason, the
380	/// system holds at most one less-atom per `(smaller, larger)` pair, so the
381	/// `eqClasses` grouping is a no-op (singleton groups) — we only need to
382	/// reproduce its SORT (by `(smaller, larger)`, via `Ord LVar`) and the
383	/// single-reason colour. The gradient/`;weight` share code only fires for
384	/// multi-reason groups, which cannot arise here.
385	fn emit_edges_merged(
386	    g: &mut DotBuilder,
387	    edges: &[GEdge],
388	    node_map: &HashMap<&LVar, &RuleACInst>,
389	    ds_nodes: &std::collections::BTreeMap<LVar, String>,
390	) {
391	    // restEdges: keep original order, drop less-edges.
392	    for edge in edges {
393	        match edge {
394	            GEdge::System(src, tgt) => {
395	                g.edge(node_map, src, tgt);
396	            }
397	            GEdge::UnsolvedChain(src, tgt) => g.chain_edge(node_map, src, tgt),
398	            GEdge::Less(_) => {}
399	        }
400	    }
401	    // lessEdges: collect, sort by (smaller, larger) like `eqClasses`, emit one
402	    // merged edge per pair.
403	    let mut lesses: Vec<&LessAtom> = edges.iter()
404	        .filter_map(|e| match e { GEdge::Less(la) => Some(la), _ => None })
405	        .collect();
406	    lesses.sort_by(|a, b| (&a.smaller, &a.larger).cmp(&(&b.smaller, &b.larger)));
407	    for la in lesses {
408	        g.less_edge(la, ds_nodes);
409	    }
410	}
411	
412	fn abbreviate_rule(
413	    ru: &RuleACInst,
414	    abbrev: &dyn Fn(&LNTerm) -> Option<LNTerm>,
415	) -> RuleACInst {
416	    let mut new_ru = ru.clone();
417	    new_ru.premises = ru.premises.iter()
418	        .map(|fa| apply_abbreviations_fact(abbrev, fa))
419	        .collect();
420	    new_ru.actions = ru.actions.iter()
421	        .map(|fa| apply_abbreviations_fact(abbrev, fa))
422	        .collect();
423	    new_ru.conclusions = ru.conclusions.iter()
424	        .map(|fa| apply_abbreviations_fact(abbrev, fa))
425	        .collect();
426	    new_ru
427	}
428	
429	/// Helper used by handlers to render the [`System`] as DOT and pipe it
430	/// through `/usr/bin/dot -Tsvg` (or whatever's on `$PATH`) under the given
431	/// graph options.  Returns the SVG bytes on success.  When `dot` is
432	/// missing or fails, returns the DOT source instead (the frontend's
433	/// `intdot-staticgraph` can render DOT client-side via viz.js, so this
434	/// stays a useful response).
435	pub fn render_svg_or_dot_with(sys: &System, opts: &GraphOptions) -> RenderResult {
436	    let dot = system_to_dot_with(sys, opts);
437	    match try_render_dot_to_svg(&dot) {
438	        Ok(svg) => RenderResult::Svg(svg),
439	        Err(_) => RenderResult::Dot(dot),
440	    }
441	}
442	
443	/// What we got back from `dot`.
444	pub enum RenderResult {
445	    /// SVG bytes produced by `dot -Tsvg`.
446	    Svg(Vec<u8>),
447	    /// Raw DOT source, returned when the `dot` binary is unavailable or failed.
448	    Dot(String),
449	}
450	
451	fn try_render_dot_to_svg(dot: &str) -> std::io::Result<Vec<u8>> {
452	    use std::io::Write;
453	    use std::process::{Command, Stdio};
454	    let mut child = Command::new("dot")
455	        .args(["-Tsvg"])
456	        .stdin(Stdio::piped())
457	        .stdout(Stdio::piped())
458	        .stderr(Stdio::piped())
459	        .spawn()?;
460	    // Write the full DOT to `dot`'s stdin on a separate thread while the
461	    // main thread drains stdout/stderr via `wait_with_output`.  Doing the
462	    // (blocking) `write_all` inline before reading stdout can deadlock on
463	    // large graphs: `dot` fills its stdout pipe and blocks, and so does our
464	    // `write_all` on a full stdin pipe.
465	    let writer = child.stdin.take().map(|mut sin| {
466	        let bytes = dot.as_bytes().to_vec();
467	        std::thread::spawn(move || sin.write_all(&bytes))
468	    });
469	    let out = child.wait_with_output()?;
470	    if let Some(handle) = writer {
471	        // Propagate any write error (ignore a panicked thread).
472	        if let Ok(res) = handle.join() {
473	            res?;
474	        }
475	    }
476	    if !out.status.success() {
477	        return Err(std::io::Error::other(
478	            format!("dot exited with status {:?}", out.status)));
479	    }
480	    Ok(out.stdout)
481	}
482	
483	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------
484	// DOT construction
485	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------
486	
487	struct DotBuilder {
488	    buf: String,
489	}
490

497	        let _ = writeln!(self.buf, "digraph G {{");
498	        if has_clusters {

500	            // attribute block for clustered graphs.

1	//! `web-clean` — a clean-room reimplementation of the response bodies produced
2	//! by the tamarin-prover interactive web UI.
3	//!
4	//! This crate reproduces the *web layer's* output: the URL route grammar, the
5	//! JSON response envelopes, and the HTML/DOT/text page templates. Content that
6	//! the prover produces (pretty-printed terms, constraint systems, proof-method
7	//! names, graph bodies) is treated as opaque input supplied by the caller — the
8	//! crate reproduces the scaffolding, escaping, links and envelopes around it.
9	//!
10	//! Everything here was derived from black-box observation of captured responses
11	//! and live probing; see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` for the observed spec.
12	//!
13	//! Module map:
14	//! * [`route`] — parse a request path into a structured route (the grammar),
15	//!   plus the top-level ([`route::Toplevel`]) surface.
16	//! * [`dispatch`] — the UI state machine: route dispatch, theory-version
17	//!   management, and response assembly over the whole surface, via a `ProverOps`
18	//!   trait.
19	//! * [`envelope`] — the three JSON response shapes (`{html,title}`, `{redirect}`,
20	//!   `{alert}`).
21	//! * [`escape`] — HTML entity escaping.
22	//! * [`page`] — the theory-view HTML shell (`overview/*`, trace and diff) and the

content.content:
### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1)
**Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is
`{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among
`[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always
present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule
renders `{info}|{concl}`).
- **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts,
  `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`).
- **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact.
- **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args;
  a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified
  against the corpus.
- **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash;
  everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal.
- **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). When a group breaks, physical lines are
  separated by `\l` (with a trailing `\l`), and each continuation line is indented
  with a ` ` run **equal to the column of the broken group's first element**
  (just after `( ` for a fact, after `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action
  list). Verified across 188 192 wrapped cells.
- **Line wrapping DECISION — RESOLVED (Session 4): a fixed-width paragraph fill,
  width = 87 columns, measured per fact from column 0.** The Round-3 claim ("first
  line 5..87, not a flat width, not derivable") is **refuted**: it *is* a flat
  width. Established by driving crafted single-node theories
  (`Out(<'a01', …, 'aN'>)`, `Out(<'aa…a', 'y'>)`) through the live server and
  sweeping the term width one column at a time:
  - **W = 87 absolute, functor-invariant.** A fact stays on one line iff its flat
    rendering is ≤ 87 columns; at 88 it breaks. Tested with functor names of length
    2, 3, 6, 10 (`Ba`,`Out`,`Fact12`,`Factabcdef`): the boundary was **always** at
    flat width 87 fits / 88 breaks, so the budget is counted from the functor's own
    column 0, *including* the functor name (a longer name simply fits fewer args).
    Corpus-consistent: 99.2 % of 190 044 physical lines have fact-content ≤ 87; the
    residual are single unbreakable atoms wider than 87 (which overflow verbatim).
  - **Greedy paragraph fill.** When a group overflows, elements pack left-to-right;
    the separator `, ` trails the element it follows and stays on the line, and the
    next element wraps when it would pass column 87. Continuation lines use the
    §3f indent. Example `Out(<'a01'…'a12'>)` (flat 91): line 0 =
    `Out( <'a01', … 'a11', ` (eleven elements + trailing `, `, ending col 83),
    `'a12'>` on the next line, then the fact's `)` on its own line.
  - **Delimiter peel — BYTE-IMPLEMENTED (Session 5).** Re-probed the boundary with
    fresh single-node sweeps (`E10..E14`, `W69..W74`, ports 3210): a **tuple's `>`**
    stays with the last element iff it fits (`Out( <'aaa(72)', 'y'>` │ `)`), else it
    peels onto its own line at the tuple's **`<` column** (`W74`: `…'y'` │ `<5sp>>`
    │ `)`); a **fact's `)` ALWAYS** peels onto its own line at **col 0** once the
    fact wraps — even when it would fit (`E12`: `'a12'>` │ `)`) — because the padded
    ` )` space is the break. An **info action-list `]`** stays attached to its last
    action. An unbreakable atom wider than the budget overflows verbatim; only the
    trailing delimiters wrap.
  - **Continuation lookahead — RESOLVED (subsumed).** The old "first line 11,
    continuation 12" claim is a mis-read: the continuation packs **greedily to the
    same width 87** as the first line (`E13`→2, `E14`→3 elements on the second line,
    each ending well under 87). There is no ±1 combinator lookahead to reproduce.
  - **Info action-list = vertical `sep`, not fill.** An overflowing info cell puts
    **one action per line** (corpus `6738eb64…`: 4 actions → 4 lines, short actions
    included), `]` on the last; whereas a tuple / fact-arg list uses the greedy
    fill. Two distinct combinators, both byte-implemented.
  - **`graph-clean::render::wrap_cell`** implements all of the above (`layout_fact`
    fill+`)`-peel, `layout_tuple` fill+`>`-peel, `layout_info` vertical `sep`,
    `run_layout` greedy fill, `split_top_commas`), wired into
    `generate::build_record`; byte-verified against the 7 captured probe fixtures
    (`wrap_E11..E14`, `wrap_W71/W72/W74`).
  - **NEW RESIDUAL — the wrap TRIGGER is accumulated-column (Wadler `group`/`fits`).**
    `wrap_cell` decides to wrap on the cell's **own flat width** > 87, which is
    correct for a cell early on its record line (all probes/fixtures). But a cell
    deep on a wide record line wraps **earlier**: measured over 61 821 corpus
    wrapped records, physical line-0 width ranges 19..277 (no fixed absolute page
    width), and a 21-char fact `Y_counter( pid.1, t )` at line-start peels its `)`
    while an identical-args instance late on a broken line stays flat — because
    tamarin's `fits` measures the flat rendering **plus the rest of the record
    line** against the remaining width. Reproducing that trigger needs the exact
    record document tree (the whole label as one `group`/`nest`/`line` Doc); the
    width (87), fill packing, peel columns, and the fill-vs-sep split are pinned.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 4. Clustering / simplification (§ priority 3)

**Trigger rule (byte-verified over all 14705 graphs, 0 violations):**
> The COMPACT clustered rendering (§2b, with `subgraph "cluster_…"` blocks) is
> used **iff the graph contains at least one node with `role` ≠ `"Undefined"`.**
> Otherwise the SIMPLE header (§2a) is used.

Non-Undefined roles arise from process/SAPIC agents (e.g. `Initiator`,
`Responder`, `server`, `Tag`, `v`). Pure multiset-rewriting theories emit the
simple header everywhere; role-annotated theories emit the compact header on
every non-empty graph (empty graphs have no role nodes ⇒ simple).

### Cluster block
```
subgraph "cluster_<Role>_Session_<k>" {
nodesep="0.6";
ranksep="0.6";
label="<Role>_Session_<k>";
style="filled";
color="#RRGGBBAA";
penwidth="2";
fillcolor="#RRGGBBAA";
overlap="false";
sep="4";
n<ID>[shape="record",label="…",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="white",role="<Role>"];

}
```
- Cluster name = `cluster_<clusterlabel>`; `label` = the clusterlabel
  (`<Role>_Session_<k>`). Nodes grouped by (role, session instance).
- `color`/`fillcolor` are an 8-hex ARGB (alpha `4C`, ~30%) hashed from the
  cluster; node `fillcolor` is the saturated per-role color (live SAPIC probe
  `cluster_Process_Session_1` had node `fillcolor="#ffffff"`, `fontcolor="black"`
  — so the node fill is not always saturated). Exact hash **[GAP]**.

### Cluster EMISSION ORDER (§ Round-5 item 1) — byte-verified over 1457 corpus files
The top-level statement order of a clustered graph is **always**
`[free nodes] [clusters] [edges] [rankblock/legend] [invis edges]`: 0 files have a
top-level node after a cluster, 0 clusters after an edge. **Clusters contain only
records** (50 453 record cells, 0 ellipse/edge). A cluster's records occupy a
**contiguous** id range and clusters are ordered by increasing id (first-appearance
== id order). Since id order == emission order (§3e), the global id counter runs
over the free (non-role) nodes first — giving them the low ids — then over the
clustered records grouped by cluster. `graph-clean::generate` reproduces this:
Pass 1 allocates ids in `System.nodes` order, routing each role-record's statement
into its `cluster(label,color)` bucket and everything else to the free top level,
then emits free nodes, then cluster blocks in first-appearance order, then edges,
then the legend. Byte-exact on a live SAPIC single-cluster graph
(`cluster_process.dot`) and the multi-cluster corpus payload
`79c16911ad179d51.dot` (4 free ellipses + 1 cluster + 2 deduction edges).

### What "simplification" means here
There is no exposed simplification *level*; the corpus reflects one fixed
rendering pipeline. The observable "simplification" is the fixed compression the
server applies before rendering: intruder-knowledge (`!KU`) nodes are drawn as
gray ellipses; single-premise/conclusion structural chains are drawn with bold
weight-10 edges; role nodes are packed into per-session clusters (compact mode).
Reproducing the *content* of that compression from a raw constraint system
requires the solver and is **[GAP]** — out of scope for a rendering crate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 5. Node abbreviation (§ priority 2)

When a graph is drawn, sufficiently complex sub-terms are replaced by short
NAMES and listed in a legend node. Observed from 113 924 legend rows.

The ported `render_balanced` reproduces HS's proportional-width layout. Let me confirm this divergence triggers on real records using the HS-ground-truth corpus, then verify with a live capture.

python3 - <<'PY'
import glob, re
# Find a record cell where a fact wraps (has \l mid-cell) but its first physical
# line's content is well under 87 cols -> accumulated-column trigger (clean would
# keep it flat). Cell text sits between <nK> and the next | or }.
hits=0
example=None
for path in glob.glob("*.dot"):
    txt=open(path, encoding='utf-8', errors='replace').read()
    # find record labels
    for m in re.finditer(r'label="(\{\{.*?\}\})"', txt):
        label=m.group(1)
        # split into cells by the port marker
        for cm in re.finditer(r'<n\d+> ([^|{}]*(?:\\l)[^|{}]*)', label):
            cell=cm.group(1)
            if '\\l' not in cell: continue
            first=cell.split('\\l')[0]
            # width of first line content (approx: unescape \< \> \{ \} \| and count)
            fl=re.sub(r'\\([<>{}|])', r'\1', first)
            w=len(fl)
            # a fact that wrapped: is it a "NAME( ... " that broke though short?
            if w < 60 and first.rstrip().endswith(',') and '(' in first:
                hits+=1
                if example is None or w < example[2]:
                    example=(path, cell[:200], w, first)
print("candidate accumulated-column wraps (first-line <60 & ends ','):", hits)
if example:
    print("file:", example[0])
    print("first-line width:", example[2])
    print("first line:", repr(example[3]))
    print("cell head:", repr(example[1]))
